summary: Jeff Atwood explores the tension between following conventions and innovating in software development. While he strongly endorses consistency in code ('do things the same way everyone else does' to aid maintainability), he argues that UI design deserves more flexibility. He contends that not all conventions are equally valuable—some are timeless while others exist simply because nobody questioned them. The key is to experiment methodically: understand the existing convention, have a reasoned argument for deviation, collect usage data, and make evidence-based decisions. Without such experimentation, we'd never have gotten innovations like TiVo's interface or Office's ribbon.
key_points: In code, always favor consistency over cleverness—the next person reading it (including future you) needs to understand it immediately
User interfaces should be internally consistent but not necessarily consistent with every other application in the world
Not all UI conventions are created equal: some are timeless, some exist by default because nobody questioned them, some outlive their usefulness
Companies like Amazon, Yahoo, and Google succeed by serving experimental features to small user segments and collecting data
UI experimentation requires understanding the convention, having reasoned arguments for deviation, collecting usage data, and making data-driven decisions
money_quotes: We should, as much as possible, try to do things the same way everyone else does them.
If you did something clever, the next person to touch it will look at the code and not immediately understand.
User interfaces should be internally consistent, but not necessarily consistent with every other application in the rest of the world.
How do we discriminate between conventions that actually help us and those that are merely... expected?
UI experimentation is not only desirable, but necessary. If we don't experiment, we can't evolve UI forward.
If you're not collecting usage data, or your reason is 'it looks better this way,' then you're doing it wrong, and you should stick with the conventions.
key_insight: Code should follow conventions religiously for maintainability, but UI conventions should be questioned and tested empirically—innovation requires experimentation, but experimentation requires data.
